characters.relation: Lover-teacher who tried to rein him
characters.blurb: Sought Alcibiades’s soul more than his beauty; saved his life in battle and stood as the one man he truly feared to shame.
Wealthy dove who opposed the Sicilian dream. Ostracism nearly took one of them; Hyperbolus fell instead—and Sicily ruined both.
Kinsman who raised Alcibiades after Clinias’s death—the standard of glory the ward both imitated and outraged.
Died at Coronea; his fame and Alcmaeonid ties left Alcibiades rich, brilliant, and dangerously unsupervised.
Daughter of Hipponicus; tried to leave Alcibiades over his courtesans, then was fetched home from the archon’s office.
Torch-bearer of the Mysteries whose wealth and daughter tied Alcibiades to the first houses of Athens.
Heir of Hipponicus; tangled with Alcibiades in money, marriage, and the gossip of high Athens.

characters.relation: Persian satrap; final host and killer
characters.blurb: Phrygian satrap who alternately helped and hunted him; Lysander finally had him arrange Alcibiades’s murder.
Persian governor in Asia whom Alcibiades dazzled and advised—playing Persia against Sparta for Athens’s sake (and his own).
Navarch who closed the war at Aegospotami and would not leave Alcibiades alive to threaten the new order.
Blunt soldier who wanted to attack Syracuse at once; died early, leaving Nicias alone with a war he hated.
Arrived to stiffen Sicily against Athens; his skill sealed the disaster Alcibiades had sold to the demos.

characters.relation: Orator tangled in the Mysteries scandal
characters.blurb: Named in the Hermae/Mysteries panic that exploded as the Sicilian fleet sailed—the storm that drove Alcibiades to flee.
Tried to ostracize Alcibiades or Nicias; they combined and ostracized him instead—the last such vote.
Third man in the political fight that ended Hyperbolus; polished speaker without Alcibiades’s force.
Host at Sparta whose wife Timaea was said to bear Alcibiades a child—personal hate atop policy.
Queen whispered to have loved Alcibiades; the scandal sharpened Agis’s enmity.
General who opposed recalling Alcibiades; later murdered after playing all sides in the 411 revolution.
Helped bring Alcibiades back to the fleet; later restorer of democracy at Athens.
Shared commands in the Ionian and Hellespontine campaigns of Alcibiades’s second Athenian career.
Fell when Alcibiades and the Athenian captains destroyed his fleet—the peak of the recall years.
Left in charge against orders, he provoked Lysander and lost ships—the excuse Athens used to cashier Alcibiades again.
Courtesan who was with him in Phrygia and wrapped his body after the assassins burned him out.
Commander entangled in the Persian–Spartan deals Alcibiades tried to manage through Tisaphernes.
Appears among the clever, dangerous young men of Alcibiades’s set—foreshadowing the Thirty.
Relative who defended Alcibiades’s interests in the assembly during the recalls and accusations.

Ward of Pericles whose beauty, wealth, and audacity made him the most desired and most dangerous man in Athens. He won great victories, betrayed every city that trusted him, and died by assassins' fire in a Phrygian village—with only Timandra to wrap his body.

characters.relation: Oligarch and rival in the revolution
characters.blurb: Played all sides in the 411 coup and its collapse; his moves against Alcibiades and Phrynichus illustrate the lethal politics of the age of the Thirty.
Wealthy tanner-politician who suffered Alcibiades's contemptuous treatment in youth—Plutarch tells the story of wine thrown in his face, which Anytus's admirers found charming rather than insulting.

The famous Athenian misanthrope who despised all mankind except Alcibiades—because, he said, the young man would one day cause great calamity to Athens. Plutarch records the prophecy with evident irony.
Popular leader who stoked the Mysteries panic to drive Alcibiades from the city before the Sicilian fleet sailed; he was himself later killed in the political violence that followed.
Pericles's brother who shared legal guardianship of Alcibiades and Clinias after their father's death—but it was Pericles's household that shaped the boy.

characters.relation: Philosopher who wrote him into the dialogues
characters.blurb: Wrote the Symposium and the two Alcibiades dialogues; his portrait of the brilliant, vain, and beloved young man is the deepest literary account Plutarch cites.

Judged Alcibiades's conduct of the war brilliantly but his private life disastrously; his cool assessment of the Sicilian expedition as genius undone by personal scandal stands behind Plutarch's account.
In the Frogs and elsewhere Aristophanes caught Athens's ambivalence about Alcibiades—that the city both longed for him and feared him, missing him as it damned him.
Brilliant speechwriter involved in the Four Hundred's revolution; named among the clever, dangerous figures whose careers intersect Alcibiades's last Athenian years.
An Athenian who gave Alcibiades money to buy a horse at Olympia; Alcibiades entered it under his own name instead—a piece of shameless appropriation Plutarch records without softening.
The Hermae—herm-pillars of Hermes—were mutilated on the eve of the Sicilian fleet's departure; the sacrilege was blamed on Alcibiades's circle and ignited the Mysteries panic.
The Eleusinian Mysteries of Demeter and Persephone were said to have been parodied at Alcibiades's private parties—the charge that made him a target for prosecution even as the fleet sailed.

characters.relation: Priestess who refused to curse him
characters.blurb: When the assembly ordered Athenian priests to curse Alcibiades formally, Theano alone refused, saying she was a priestess to pray, not to curse—a moment of quiet dignity Plutarch admires.

characters.relation: Wealthy host of the Mysteries parody
characters.blurb: Named alongside others at whose houses the sacred Eleusinian rites were reportedly mocked—the social network of the scandal that drove Alcibiades to defect to Sparta.
